demos.claim_a: Metformin is contraindicated when eGFR falls below 45 mL/min/1.73m².
demos.claim_b: Metformin may be continued at a reduced dose until eGFR drops below 30 mL/min/1.73m².
demos.reasoning: The claims conflict on the eGFR threshold for metformin use. Claim A says metformin is contraindicated once eGFR is below 45 mL/min/1.73m², while Claim B says it can be continued at a reduced dose until eGFR is below 30 mL/min/1.73m². These cannot both be true as a general rule because they prescribe different stopping points for the same medication under the same condition.
demos.extracted_entities: Metformin; eGFR; kidney function threshold; reduced dose
demos.extracted_conditions:
Claim A: when eGFR falls below 45 mL/min/1.73m².
Claim B: until eGFR drops below 30 mL/min/1.73m²; continued at reduced dose.
demos.extracted_actions:
Claim A: contraindicate metformin.
Claim B: continue metformin at a reduced dose.

demos.claim_a: Patients on basal insulin with HbA1c above 9.0% must be referred to endocrinology within 30 days.
demos.claim_b: Endocrinology referral for insulin-managed patients is indicated only when HbA1c exceeds 10.0% on two consecutive measurements.
demos.reasoning: The claims set different referral thresholds for overlapping patients on insulin. Claim A requires referral within 30 days when HbA1c is above 9.0%. Claim B says referral is indicated only when HbA1c exceeds 10.0% on two consecutive measurements. For patients with HbA1c between 9.0% and 10.0%, Claim A requires referral but Claim B does not. Because they impose incompatible conditions for when referral is required, they contradict each other on the same topic.
demos.extracted_entities: Patients on basal insulin; endocrinology; insulin-managed patients; HbA1c; referral timing (within 30 days); consecutive measurements
demos.extracted_conditions:
Claim A: applies to patients on basal insulin with HbA1c above 9.0%.
Claim B: applies only when HbA1c exceeds 10.0% on two consecutive measurements.
Overlapping scope: insulin-treated / insulin-managed patients.
Temporal condition in A: referral within 30 days.
demos.extracted_actions:
Claim A: must be referred to endocrinology within 30 days.
Claim B: endocrinology referral is indicated.

demos.claim_a: Uncomplicated urinary tract infections in non-pregnant adults are treated with a 3-day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole.
demos.claim_b: Standard antibiotic therapy for uncomplicated UTI requires a minimum 7-day treatment course.
demos.reasoning: The claims conflict on the required antibiotic treatment duration for uncomplicated UTI in non-pregnant adults. Claim A says a 3-day course is used, while Claim B says standard therapy requires at least 7 days. For the same condition, these durations cannot both be the general requirement.
demos.extracted_entities: uncomplicated urinary tract infections, non-pregnant adults, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, standard antibiotic therapy
demos.extracted_conditions: Claim A: applies to uncomplicated UTIs in non-pregnant adults. Claim B: applies to uncomplicated UTI under standard antibiotic therapy. Both concern the same general condition, with no qualifying exception that reconciles 3 days vs minimum 7 days.
demos.extracted_actions: Treat with a 3-day course of trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole; require a minimum 7-day antibiotic treatment course.

demos.claim_a: Post-surgical patients must complete a minimum 24-hour in-hospital observation period before discharge.
demos.claim_b: Patients undergoing same-day ambulatory procedures classified as ASA Class I or II may be discharged within 6 hours of procedure completion if all vital sign criteria are met.
demos.reasoning: The claims are conditionally compatible. Claim A sets a general rule for post-surgical patients: a minimum 24-hour in-hospital observation before discharge. Claim B carves out a narrower subset—same-day ambulatory procedures for ASA Class I or II patients—who may be discharged within 6 hours if vital sign criteria are met. Since B applies only to a specific subgroup and includes extra conditions, it does not inherently contradict the broader rule unless A explicitly forbids exceptions.
demos.extracted_entities: Post-surgical patients; same-day ambulatory procedures; ASA Class I or II patients; in-hospital observation period; discharge; vital sign criteria
demos.extracted_conditions:
- Claim A: Applies to post-surgical patients generally.
- Claim B: Applies only to patients undergoing same-day ambulatory procedures classified as ASA Class I or II.
- Claim B: Discharge within 6 hours is allowed only if all vital sign criteria are met.
- Claim B: Exception is limited to the described subset.
demos.extracted_actions:
- Claim A: Must complete a minimum 24-hour in-hospital observation period before discharge.
- Claim B: May be discharged within 6 hours of procedure completion if conditions are satisfied.

demos.claim_a: Antibiotic prophylaxis with cefazolin 2g IV is mandatory for all patients undergoing cardiac valve replacement surgery.
demos.claim_b: Patients with a documented penicillin or cephalosporin allergy presenting for cardiac valve surgery must receive clindamycin 600 mg IV as the prophylactic antibiotic substitution.
demos.reasoning: The claims are conditionally compatible rather than contradictory. Claim A sets a general rule that cefazolin 2g IV is mandatory for all patients undergoing cardiac valve replacement surgery. Claim B specifies an exception/substitution for patients with documented penicillin or cephalosporin allergy, requiring clindamycin 600 mg IV instead. Since B applies only to a subset of patients for whom cefazolin may not be appropriate, the two claims can coexist.
demos.extracted_entities: cardiac valve replacement surgery; patients; cefazolin 2g IV; documented penicillin allergy; documented cephalosporin allergy; clindamycin 600 mg IV
demos.extracted_conditions:
Claim A: applies to all patients undergoing cardiac valve replacement surgery.
Claim B: applies only to patients with a documented penicillin or cephalosporin allergy presenting for cardiac valve surgery.
demos.extracted_actions:
Claim A: administer mandatory cefazolin 2g IV prophylaxis.
Claim B: administer clindamycin 600 mg IV as the prophylactic antibiotic substitution.

demos.claim_a: All Type 2 diabetic patients must have HbA1c measured every 90 days.
demos.claim_b: Patients with well-controlled Type 2 diabetes, defined as two consecutive HbA1c readings below 7.0%, require HbA1c measurement every 180 days.
demos.reasoning: The claims specify different HbA1c testing intervals for overlapping Type 2 diabetic patients. Claim A states a universal requirement of every 90 days for all Type 2 diabetic patients. Claim B gives a more specific rule for well-controlled Type 2 diabetes, requiring testing every 180 days. Since a patient can satisfy the condition in claim B and still be within the class covered by claim A, the two claims prescribe different schedules for the same subgroup. However, because one could interpret claim B as a conditional exception to the general rule, they are not inherently contradictory in all scenarios unless exclusivity is assumed.
demos.extracted_entities: Type 2 diabetic patients; well-controlled Type 2 diabetes patients; HbA1c testing/measurement; consecutive HbA1c readings below 7.0%; 90-day interval; 180-day interval
demos.extracted_conditions:
Claim A: applies to all Type 2 diabetic patients, no stated exceptions.
Claim B: applies only to patients with well-controlled Type 2 diabetes, defined as two consecutive HbA1c readings below 7.0%.
Claim B also overrides the base schedule for that subgroup by specifying a 180-day interval.
demos.extracted_actions:
Claim A: measure HbA1c every 90 days.
Claim B: measure HbA1c every 180 days for well-controlled Type 2 diabetic patients.

demos.claim_a: Prior authorization is required before ordering an MRI for musculoskeletal complaints.
demos.claim_b: MRI orders for suspected acute spinal cord compression must be placed immediately without prior authorization and completed within 2 hours of the clinical decision.
demos.reasoning: The claims overlap on MRI ordering policy but apply under different conditions. Claim A states a general rule: prior authorization is required before ordering an MRI for musculoskeletal complaints. Claim B gives a specific exception for suspected acute spinal cord compression: MRI must be ordered immediately without prior authorization and completed within 2 hours. Since a specific emergency exception can coexist with a general prior-authorization rule, they are conditionally compatible, not inherently contradictory.
demos.extracted_entities: MRI orders, prior authorization, musculoskeletal complaints, suspected acute spinal cord compression, clinical decision
demos.extracted_conditions:
Claim A: applies to MRI orders for musculoskeletal complaints; prior authorization required before ordering.
Claim B: applies to suspected acute spinal cord compression; MRI must be ordered immediately, no prior authorization, and completed within 2 hours of the clinical decision.
demos.extracted_actions:
Claim A: obtain prior authorization before ordering an MRI.
Claim B: place the MRI order immediately without prior authorization; complete the MRI within 2 hours.

demos.claim_a: The standard post-discharge follow-up window for Type 2 diabetes patients is 4 weeks.
demos.claim_b: Patients discharged following a hypoglycemic episode that required IV dextrose administration must schedule follow-up within 72 hours of discharge.
demos.reasoning: The claims are compatible because claim_a states a standard follow-up window for Type 2 diabetes patients, while claim_b gives a more specific and stricter follow-up requirement for a subset of patients discharged after a hypoglycemic episode requiring IV dextrose. The second claim can be treated as an exception or special-case rule within the broader 4-week standard, so they do not inherently contradict.
demos.extracted_entities: Type 2 diabetes patients; post-discharge follow-up window; hypoglycemic episode; IV dextrose administration; discharge; follow-up scheduling
demos.extracted_conditions: Claim_a applies to standard post-discharge follow-up for Type 2 diabetes patients. Claim_b applies specifically to patients discharged after a hypoglycemic episode that required IV dextrose administration. Claim_b requires follow-up within 72 hours of discharge, regardless of the general standard window.
demos.extracted_actions: Set standard post-discharge follow-up window to 4 weeks for Type 2 diabetes patients. Schedule follow-up within 72 hours for patients discharged after hypoglycemic episodes requiring IV dextrose.

signature.instructions: Evaluate if two claims logically contradict each other. Pay strict attention to conditional constraints (e.g., 'only if', 'unless', 'strictly avoid'). If one statement is conditional and the other is a general rule, they might NOT contradict.

fields.description: The core entity or topic being discussed
Extract conditional constraints from both claims
Extract prescribed actions from both claims
Return strictly 'True' if they inherently contradict in all scenarios, or 'False' if they are conditionally compatible.
